title: Impact of air pollution on pulmonary function and respiratory symptoms in children. Longitudinal repeated-measures study.
ehr_evidence: The study recruited a cohort of 464 children and obtained measurements directly (spirometry, symptoms, pollutants) rather than using EHR data.
summary: A longitudinal repeated-measures study investigating the impact of air pollution on pulmonary function and respiratory symptoms in school-age children in Salamanca, Mexico.
key_findings: Abnormalities in lung function and respiratory symptoms were higher in the school closer to pollution sources.
PM10 levels were the most consistent factor with a negative relationship with FVC, FEV1, and PEF.
Differences in health effects disappeared in winter due to generalized air pollution.
captured_features: Air pollutants (O3, SO2, NO, NO2, NOx, PM10)
population: 464 children (6-14 years old) in Salamanca, Mexico
